role_summary: Civil rights attorney with the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee (LCDC) in Louisiana 1965-1972; counsel for Gary Duncan in Duncan v. Louisiana, the case Sobol argued at the United States Supreme Court in January 1968 that established for the first time a Sixth Amendment right to a jury trial in state criminal proceedings; arrested in February 1967 in Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana on charges of practicing law without a license, the case at the heart of the 2020 documentary A Crime on the Bayou.
ai_reading: Sobol's top semantic neighbor is the joint John and Jean Rosenberg interview (entry 61) at a high cosine count of 318, a strong single-neighbor signature in this catalog. The Rosenberg pairing is the catalog's clearest civil-rights-attorney-and-attorney-couple cosine; John and Jean Rosenberg both practiced civil rights law in Mississippi during the same 1960s LCDC-aligned tradition Sobol practiced in Louisiana, and the embedding catches the shared cause-lawyer vocabulary across state lines as a stronger signal than either's pairing with their own state's other organizers. The catalog flags a -0.76 reading on the Sacred Framing vs Secular Framing axis (far toward Sacred) as the most-distinctive structural reading; for a Jewish civil rights attorney whose work was institutionally secular (constitutional law, federal civil rights litigation), the embedding's pull toward Sacred Framing is the catalog's most-mismatched lawyer-page reading on this axis, possibly catching theological and moral-vocabulary registers in his transcript that the formal legal-record framings filter out.
biographical_paragraph: While Sobol's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), his external biographical record is well-documented [src: 1, src: 2]. Sobol was born May 29, 1937 in New York City. He grew up on the West Side of Manhattan, graduated from the Bronx High School of Science in 1954, took a Bachelor's at Union College in Schenectady (1958) and a JD from Columbia Law School in 1961, where he ranked third in his class and served as Notes and Comments Editor of the Law Review [src: 2]. He clerked for Judge Paul Hays on the Second Circuit and for FTC Commissioner Philip Elman, then joined Arnold, Fortas & Porter in Washington, D.C. In summer 1965 he volunteered with the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee (LCDC) in Louisiana; what was intended as a three-week assignment became a fifty-year civil rights legal practice [src: 2]. In summer 1966 he took a leave from the firm, moved his family to New Orleans, and worked full-time for the LCDC for three years across Louisiana federal and state courts on school-desegregation, voter-registration, and civil rights-protester defense cases. He represented Gary Duncan in Duncan v. Louisiana, arguing the case at the Supreme Court in January 1968; the May 1968 decision established for the first time a Sixth Amendment right to jury trial in state criminal proceedings [src: 2]. In February 1967, while still representing Duncan, Sobol was arrested in Plaquemines Parish on charges of practicing law without a license, an arrest directed by arch-segregationist parish boss Leander Perez; the LCDC's federal action against Perez (the Sobol v. Perez case, 1968) became a landmark in cross-state-bar civil rights legal practice [src: 2]. Sobol continued civil rights and consumer-protection litigation through the next five decades, including the Dalkon Shield bankruptcy case for which his 1991 book won the 1992 American Bar Association Silver Gavel Award. He died March 24, 2020 at eighty-two [src: 2].
movement_context: The Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee (LCDC) was founded in June 1964 by Henry Schwarzschild as an ACLU-affiliated emergency legal-defense apparatus assembled for the Mississippi Freedom Summer voter-registration campaign, drawing volunteer attorneys from the ACLU, the American Jewish Congress, the Congress of Racial Equality, the NAACP, and the National Council of Churches into a single Jackson-based coordinating office; the LCDC formally separated from the constituent organizations in 1965 and continued through the late 1960s as the principal volunteer-attorney pipeline for southern civil rights criminal-defense work [src: 3]. The LCDC's institutional distinction from the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund (LDF) is that the LDF concentrated on civil rights affirmative litigation (school desegregation, voting rights, employment discrimination, public-accommodations enforcement), while the LCDC concentrated on the criminal defense of civil rights demonstrators, organizers, and field workers facing state-court prosecution for the direct-action campaigns themselves; the two organizations were complementary and often coordinated counsel on the same underlying organizing situations [src: 3]. The Bogalusa-Plaquemines federal litigation in which Sobol became the named plaintiff was the doctrinal apex of the LCDC's cross-state-bar legal-practice posture. After Sobol's February 1967 arrest in Plaquemines Parish on a state criminal complaint for practicing law without a Louisiana license, the LCDC filed a federal civil rights action in the Eastern District of Louisiana seeking to enjoin Leander Perez's prosecution; the three-judge federal district court in Sobol v. Perez, 289 F. Supp. 392 (E.D. La. 1968), held that the Louisiana practice-of-law statute as applied to out-of-state civil rights cooperating attorneys violated the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the First and Fourteenth Amendments, and enjoined the prosecution; the ruling became the controlling federal precedent on cross-state-bar civil rights legal practice and the basis for later state-bar pro hac vice reform in the southern federal districts [src: 4]. The clientele Sobol defended during the LCDC Louisiana years were the same Bogalusa-anchored organizers documented in the Andersons (entry 25) and Bennett-Breaux-Jenkins (entry 46) catalog pages: members of the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League, the CORE Bogalusa field staff, and the Deacons of Defense and Justice chapter Fletcher Anderson helped found, plus the parallel Plaquemines Parish school-desegregation and voter-registration plaintiffs whose cases Leander Perez's parish apparatus had been suppressing for two decades [src: 5].
legacy_and_reception: After the LCDC years in Louisiana wound down in 1972, Sobol returned to private practice in Washington, D.C. but continued civil rights and public-interest litigation as a substantial fraction of his caseload through the next four decades [src: 2]. He represented claimants and represented the court-appointed claimants committee in the A. H. Robins Company / Dalkon Shield bankruptcy case in the 1980s, a mass-tort proceeding involving roughly two hundred thousand women injured by the contraceptive device; his 1991 University of Chicago Press book Bending the Law: The Story of the Dalkon Shield Bankruptcy won the 1992 American Bar Association Silver Gavel Award for legal writing [src: 2]. The 2020 documentary feature A Crime on the Bayou, directed by Nancy Buirski and released the year of Sobol's death, reconstructed the Duncan v. Louisiana and Sobol v. Perez record from contemporaneous interviews with Sobol and Gary Duncan and brought the Plaquemines Parish prosecution back into public attention; the film treated the Sobol-Duncan attorney-client relationship as the through-line of a single decade-long litigation arc rather than two separate cases [src: 6]. Sobol died March 24, 2020 in Washington, D.C. at the age of eighty-two; the death notice in the New York Times described the Plaquemines arrest, the Supreme Court argument in Duncan, and the federal injunction against Perez as the central legal achievements of his career [src: 7].

gallery.alt: Leander Henry Perez, 1914 Tulane University yearbook portrait
gallery.caption: Leander Henry Perez (1891-1969), portrait photograph from the 1914 Jambalaya, the Tulane University yearbook, page 105. Perez was the Plaquemines Parish district attorney and parish boss who directed Sobol's February 1967 arrest on charges of practicing law without a Louisiana license; the resulting federal action, Sobol v. Perez, 289 F. Supp. 392 (E.D. La. 1968), enjoined the prosecution and became the controlling federal precedent on cross-state-bar civil rights legal practice.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from the 1914 Tulane Jambalaya yearbook)
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication prior to January 1, 1931)

gallery.alt: Plaquemines Parish Courthouse, Pointe a la Hache, Louisiana, September 2009
gallery.caption: The upriver corner of the Plaquemines Parish Courthouse in Pointe a la Hache, Louisiana, photographed September 14, 2009 by Infrogmation of New Orleans. The Plaquemines Parish courthouse complex was the venue of the February 1967 criminal proceedings against Sobol on charges of practicing law without a Louisiana license, the proceedings the LCDC enjoined in federal court in Sobol v. Perez.

gallery.alt: Promised Land, the Leander Perez house on Highway 39, Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana, 2012
gallery.caption: Promised Land, the Plaquemines Parish residence of Leander Perez on Highway 39, photographed March 10, 2012 by Infrogmation of New Orleans. Perez's three-decade Plaquemines Parish boss rule from the late 1930s through the late 1960s was the institutional context for the February 1967 arrest of Sobol; the federal injunction in Sobol v. Perez was one of the first sustained federal-court interventions against the Perez parish apparatus.
